title: Autoimmune Hepatitis
oneLiner: Chronic immune-mediated liver inflammation causing hepatitis, fibrosis, and cirrhosis; treatable but potentially fatal if missed; presents in women > men, often with other autoimmune diseases.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute liver failure (ALF):** Suspect if INR >1.5 + any degree of encephalopathy (even mild confusion, asterixis). The single finding: **a rising INR despite normalising bilirubin** is a false reassurement – call hepatology immediately.
- **Oesophageal variceal haemorrhage:** In known/unknown cirrhosis presenting with haematemesis or melaena – **do not pass endoscopy; resuscitate and call GI reg for urgent OGD**. Single finding: **haematemesis + tachycardia + known liver disease**.
- **Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP):** Any cirrhotic with new ascites + fever or abdominal pain – **diagnostic paracentesis within 30 minutes**. Single finding: **a tender, distended abdomen with >250 PMN/µL in ascitic fluid**.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Fatigue, jaundice, right upper quadrant pain, arthralgia, amenorrhea (in premenopausal women). Often insidious; 25% present acutely with jaundice.
- **Key exam findings:** Hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, spider naevi, palmar erythema, ascites, asterixis (if decompensated). Look for extrahepatic autoimmune stigmata: Hashimoto’s goitre, vitiligo, rheumatoid arthritis.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Drug-induced liver injury (DILI):** History of new medication/supplement; eosinophilia; positive rechallenge (if available). **Splitter:** Normal IgG + negative autoantibodies → DILI; high IgG + positive ANA/ASMA → AIH.
- **Viral hepatitis (A, B, C, E, EBV, CMV):** Serology positive; acute HAV/HEV risk from travel; HBV can flare on steroids. **Splitter:** Check IgM anti-HAV, HBsAg, anti-HCV, EBV VCA IgM, CMV IgM.
- **Primary biliary cholangitis (PBC):** Anti-mitochondrial antibody (AMA) positive; high ALP/GAMMA-GT disproportionately > ALT. **Splitter:** AMA positive + pruritus → PBC; ANA/ASMA positive → AIH.
- **Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC):** ERCP/MRCP shows strictures; UC-associated; p-ANCA positive. **Splitter:** Cholestatic LFTs + IBD → PSC.
- **Wilson’s disease (young <40):** Low caeruloplasmin, Kayser-Fleischer rings, Coombs-negative haemolysis. **Splitter:** If <40 + neurological symptoms → check caeruloplasmin.
- **Non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH):** Metabolic syndrome; steatosis on imaging; negative autoantibodies.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Urine dip (bilirubin), blood glucose (if encephalopathy), capillary ammonia (if confusion; not mandatory). Ascitic tap if ascites – send for WBC, differential, culture, albumin.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT, bilirubin), albumin, INR/PT, glucose. Viral serology: HBsAg, anti-HCV, HAV IgM, HEV IgM, EBV VCA IgM, CMV IgM. Autoimmune panel: ANA, anti-smooth muscle (ASMA), anti-LKM1 (type 2 AIH, children), anti-mitochondrial antibody (AMA). IgG, IgA, IgM levels. Ferritin, transferrin saturation (haemochromatosis screen), caeruloplasmin, alpha-1 antitrypsin. Pregnancy test in all women of childbearing age.
- **Imaging:** First-line: **Abdominal ultrasound** – assess liver echotexture (cirrhosis?), splenomegaly, ascites, exclude biliary obstruction. Elastography (FibroScan) for fibrosis stage if clinically stable. MRCP if cholestasis dominates.
- **Special tests:** **Liver biopsy** is gold standard – shows interface hepatitis, plasma cell infiltration. Use simplified IAIHG score (points for autoantibodies ≥1:40 = 1; IgG > upper limit normal = 1; histology = 1–2; viral exclusion = 2). Score ≥7 = definite AIH; 6 = probable. **Do not delay biopsy for typical cases if coagulopathy allows**; transjugular route if coagulopathic.
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** before starting azathioprine or mycophenolate (teratogenic). Also **variceal screening OGD** if cirrhosis suspected (even without bleeding).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If ALF (INR >1.5 + encephalopathy): **call hepatology/ICU immediately**. Consider IV N-acetylcysteine (hepatology decision). Avoid sedation. Nil by mouth if encephalopathic.
  - If variceal bleed: **resuscitate with blood/FFP/platelets; IV terlipressin 0.5–1 mg; prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1 g IV); arrange urgent OGD**.
  - If SBP: **IV antibiotics (cefotaxime 2 g IV) + IV albumin 0.5-1 g/kg on day 1**.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Start immunosuppression once viral serology negative and no active sepsis. **Prednisolone** monotherapy if severe (ALT >10x ULN, bilirubin >3x ULN) or consider budesonide if mild (but budesonide only for non-cirrhotic).
  - **Azathioprine** added 2-4 weeks after prednisolone once bilirubin falling; check TPMT before first dose.
  - Monitor daily: LFT, INR, glucose (steroid-induced), blood pressure.
  - VTE prophylaxis: **enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily** (if platelets >50 and no coagulopathy – discuss with senior if INR >2.0).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Prednisolone taper over 6-12 weeks to lowest effective dose (usually 5-10 mg daily). Azathioprine 1-2 mg/kg/day maintenance.
  - Lifelong follow-up with hepatology. Annual ultrasound + AFP (HCC surveillance if cirrhosis).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline 1 L/8h or 1 L/12h if ascitic; avoid dextrose unless hypoglycaemic.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if safe).
  - Diet: normal, low sodium if ascites (<2 g/day).
  - Observations: 4-6 hourly NEWS2 initially; if encephalopathy or bleeding, 1-2 hourly.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**
  - Prednisolone: **1 mg/kg/day orally (max 60 mg)** for 2-4 weeks, then taper by 5 mg/week. **Check local policy for exact taper schedule.**
  - Azathioprine: **1-2 mg/kg/day orally** (start 50 mg daily, increase after TPMT result). Most common: 75-150 mg daily. **Never prescribe without TPMT genotype/phenotype.**
- **Alternatives:**
  - Budesonide: **9 mg daily (3 mg tds)** – use only in non-cirrhotic, mild AIH (ALT <5x ULN, bilirubin normal). Avoid if decompensated.
  - Mycophenolate mofetil: **1 g twice daily** – for azathioprine intolerance or non-response. Check baseline FBC, renal function.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Azathioprine: avoid with allopurinol (severe myelosuppression). Reduce dose if concurrent febuxostat.
  - Prednisolone: avoid live vaccines; monitor glucose, BP, bone density.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Azathioprine: dose reduction in severe renal impairment (GFR <30) – [check local policy]. No adjustment needed for hepatic impairment alone.
  - Mycophenolate: dose reduction in renal impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Azathioprine: failing to check TPMT prior to starting** – can cause life-threatening pancytopenia. Also, **giving full dose allopurinol with azathioprine** (even 1% of normal dose can cause toxicity).
  - **Steroids: giving high-dose IV prednisolone without excluding active infection** (especially viral hepatitis B – can get HBV flare).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Acute liver failure, variceal haemorrhage, SBP, steroid-induced hyperglycaemia, psychosis, pancreatitis (rare).
- **Chronic:** Cirrhosis, portal hypertension, HCC, osteoporosis (steroid-related), azathioprine hepatotoxicity (veno-occlusive disease – rare but monitor LFTs).
sections.Prognosis: - With treatment: 80–90% achieve remission; 10-year survival >80%. Without treatment: 5-year mortality ~50% in severe cases. Relapse common on dose reduction – may need prolonged low-dose steroids.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check viral hepatitis serology (HBsAg, anti-HCV) before starting steroids** – HBV can flare and cause fatal hepatitis.
- **If you suspect AIH, arrange liver biopsy even if autoantibodies are positive** – it confirms diagnosis and stages fibrosis.
- **Start PPI (omeprazole 20 mg daily) with high-dose prednisolone** to reduce risk of GI bleeding.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give azathioprine without checking TPMT** – severe bone marrow suppression.
- **Do not stop steroids abruptly** – AIH can relapse causing life-threatening hepatitis. Taper slowly over weeks.
- **Do not start steroids if viral hepatitis or DILI is still possible** – wait for serology results unless urgent (in consultation with hepatology).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Jaundice worsening or urine becoming dark.
  - New abdominal distension, leg swelling, or black/tarry stools.
  - Confusion, drowsiness, or vomiting blood.
- **Follow-up:**
  - Hepatology clinic in 2-4 weeks. Check LFT, INR, FBC at each visit until stable.
  - Pre-discharge ensure TPMT result is back if azathioprine prescribed.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - INR >2.0 and rising (regardless of bilirubin trend).
  - New confusion or GCS <15 – suspect encephalopathy.
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >4 mmol/L.
  - Acute variceal bleed – call GI registrar and ICU if unstable.
  - Ascitic tap confirms SBP (>250 PMN/µL) – start antibiotics and consult hepatology.

rawMarkdown:
## Autoimmune Hepatitis

**One-liner:** Chronic immune-mediated liver inflammation causing hepatitis, fibrosis, and cirrhosis; treatable but potentially fatal if missed; presents in women > men, often with other autoimmune diseases.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute liver failure (ALF):** Suspect if INR >1.5 + any degree of encephalopathy (even mild confusion, asterixis). The single finding: **a rising INR despite normalising bilirubin** is a false reassurement – call hepatology immediately.
- **Oesophageal variceal haemorrhage:** In known/unknown cirrhosis presenting with haematemesis or melaena – **do not pass endoscopy; resuscitate and call GI reg for urgent OGD**. Single finding: **haematemesis + tachycardia + known liver disease**.
- **Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP):** Any cirrhotic with new ascites + fever or abdominal pain – **diagnostic paracentesis within 30 minutes**. Single finding: **a tender, distended abdomen with >250 PMN/µL in ascitic fluid**.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Fatigue, jaundice, right upper quadrant pain, arthralgia, amenorrhea (in premenopausal women). Often insidious; 25% present acutely with jaundice.
- **Key exam findings:** Hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, spider naevi, palmar erythema, ascites, asterixis (if decompensated). Look for extrahepatic autoimmune stigmata: Hashimoto’s goitre, vitiligo, rheumatoid arthritis.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Drug-induced liver injury (DILI):** History of new medication/supplement; eosinophilia; positive rechallenge (if available). **Splitter:** Normal IgG + negative autoantibodies → DILI; high IgG + positive ANA/ASMA → AIH.
- **Viral hepatitis (A, B, C, E, EBV, CMV):** Serology positive; acute HAV/HEV risk from travel; HBV can flare on steroids. **Splitter:** Check IgM anti-HAV, HBsAg, anti-HCV, EBV VCA IgM, CMV IgM.
- **Primary biliary cholangitis (PBC):** Anti-mitochondrial antibody (AMA) positive; high ALP/GAMMA-GT disproportionately > ALT. **Splitter:** AMA positive + pruritus → PBC; ANA/ASMA positive → AIH.
- **Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC):** ERCP/MRCP shows strictures; UC-associated; p-ANCA positive. **Splitter:** Cholestatic LFTs + IBD → PSC.
- **Wilson’s disease (young <40):** Low caeruloplasmin, Kayser-Fleischer rings, Coombs-negative haemolysis. **Splitter:** If <40 + neurological symptoms → check caeruloplasmin.
- **Non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH):** Metabolic syndrome; steatosis on imaging; negative autoantibodies.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Urine dip (bilirubin), blood glucose (if encephalopathy), capillary ammonia (if confusion; not mandatory). Ascitic tap if ascites – send for WBC, differential, culture, albumin.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT, bilirubin), albumin, INR/PT, glucose. Viral serology: HBsAg, anti-HCV, HAV IgM, HEV IgM, EBV VCA IgM, CMV IgM. Autoimmune panel: ANA, anti-smooth muscle (ASMA), anti-LKM1 (type 2 AIH, children), anti-mitochondrial antibody (AMA). IgG, IgA, IgM levels. Ferritin, transferrin saturation (haemochromatosis screen), caeruloplasmin, alpha-1 antitrypsin. Pregnancy test in all women of childbearing age.
- **Imaging:** First-line: **Abdominal ultrasound** – assess liver echotexture (cirrhosis?), splenomegaly, ascites, exclude biliary obstruction. Elastography (FibroScan) for fibrosis stage if clinically stable. MRCP if cholestasis dominates.
- **Special tests:** **Liver biopsy** is gold standard – shows interface hepatitis, plasma cell infiltration. Use simplified IAIHG score (points for autoantibodies ≥1:40 = 1; IgG > upper limit normal = 1; histology = 1–2; viral exclusion = 2). Score ≥7 = definite AIH; 6 = probable. **Do not delay biopsy for typical cases if coagulopathy allows**; transjugular route if coagulopathic.
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** before starting azathioprine or mycophenolate (teratogenic). Also **variceal screening OGD** if cirrhosis suspected (even without bleeding).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If ALF (INR >1.5 + encephalopathy): **call hepatology/ICU immediately**. Consider IV N-acetylcysteine (hepatology decision). Avoid sedation. Nil by mouth if encephalopathic.
  - If variceal bleed: **resuscitate with blood/FFP/platelets; IV terlipressin 0.5–1 mg; prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1 g IV); arrange urgent OGD**.
  - If SBP: **IV antibiotics (cefotaxime 2 g IV) + IV albumin 0.5-1 g/kg on day 1**.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Start immunosuppression once viral serology negative and no active sepsis. **Prednisolone** monotherapy if severe (ALT >10x ULN, bilirubin >3x ULN) or consider budesonide if mild (but budesonide only for non-cirrhotic).
  - **Azathioprine** added 2-4 weeks after prednisolone once bilirubin falling; check TPMT before first dose.
  - Monitor daily: LFT, INR, glucose (steroid-induced), blood pressure.
  - VTE prophylaxis: **enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily** (if platelets >50 and no coagulopathy – discuss with senior if INR >2.0).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Prednisolone taper over 6-12 weeks to lowest effective dose (usually 5-10 mg daily). Azathioprine 1-2 mg/kg/day maintenance.
  - Lifelong follow-up with hepatology. Annual ultrasound + AFP (HCC surveillance if cirrhosis).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline 1 L/8h or 1 L/12h if ascitic; avoid dextrose unless hypoglycaemic.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if safe).
  - Diet: normal, low sodium if ascites (<2 g/day).
  - Observations: 4-6 hourly NEWS2 initially; if encephalopathy or bleeding, 1-2 hourly.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**
  - Prednisolone: **1 mg/kg/day orally (max 60 mg)** for 2-4 weeks, then taper by 5 mg/week. **Check local policy for exact taper schedule.**
  - Azathioprine: **1-2 mg/kg/day orally** (start 50 mg daily, increase after TPMT result). Most common: 75-150 mg daily. **Never prescribe without TPMT genotype/phenotype.**
- **Alternatives:**
  - Budesonide: **9 mg daily (3 mg tds)** – use only in non-cirrhotic, mild AIH (ALT <5x ULN, bilirubin normal). Avoid if decompensated.
  - Mycophenolate mofetil: **1 g twice daily** – for azathioprine intolerance or non-response. Check baseline FBC, renal function.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Azathioprine: avoid with allopurinol (severe myelosuppression). Reduce dose if concurrent febuxostat.
  - Prednisolone: avoid live vaccines; monitor glucose, BP, bone density.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Azathioprine: dose reduction in severe renal impairment (GFR <30) – [check local policy]. No adjustment needed for hepatic impairment alone.
  - Mycophenolate: dose reduction in renal impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Azathioprine: failing to check TPMT prior to starting** – can cause life-threatening pancytopenia. Also, **giving full dose allopurinol with azathioprine** (even 1% of normal dose can cause toxicity).
  - **Steroids: giving high-dose IV prednisolone without excluding active infection** (especially viral hepatitis B – can get HBV flare).

### Complications
- **Acute:** Acute liver failure, variceal haemorrhage, SBP, steroid-induced hyperglycaemia, psychosis, pancreatitis (rare).
- **Chronic:** Cirrhosis, portal hypertension, HCC, osteoporosis (steroid-related), azathioprine hepatotoxicity (veno-occlusive disease – rare but monitor LFTs).

### Prognosis
- With treatment: 80–90% achieve remission; 10-year survival >80%. Without treatment: 5-year mortality ~50% in severe cases. Relapse common on dose reduction – may need prolonged low-dose steroids.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check viral hepatitis serology (HBsAg, anti-HCV) before starting steroids** – HBV can flare and cause fatal hepatitis.
- **If you suspect AIH, arrange liver biopsy even if autoantibodies are positive** – it confirms diagnosis and stages fibrosis.
- **Start PPI (omeprazole 20 mg daily) with high-dose prednisolone** to reduce risk of GI bleeding.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give azathioprine without checking TPMT** – severe bone marrow suppression.
- **Do not stop steroids abruptly** – AIH can relapse causing life-threatening hepatitis. Taper slowly over weeks.
- **Do not start steroids if viral hepatitis or DILI is still possible** – wait for serology results unless urgent (in consultation with hepatology).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Jaundice worsening or urine becoming dark.
  - New abdominal distension, leg swelling, or black/tarry stools.
  - Confusion, drowsiness, or vomiting blood.
- **Follow-up:**
  - Hepatology clinic in 2-4 weeks. Check LFT, INR, FBC at each visit until stable.
  - Pre-discharge ensure TPMT result is back if azathioprine prescribed.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - INR >2.0 and rising (regardless of bilirubin trend).
  - New confusion or GCS <15 – suspect encephalopathy.
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >4 mmol/L.
  - Acute variceal bleed – call GI registrar and ICU if unstable.
  - Ascitic tap confirms SBP (>250 PMN/µL) – start antibiotics and consult hepatology.
